result: I keep thinking about the hour just before dawn, when the city forgets itself. Streetlights hum a shade paler, traffic lights click red-amber-green for no audience, and even the river slows its breathing. In that hush, the concrete smells like wet chalk, and every crosswalk is an empty piano waiting for footsteps to play it. I walk barefoot on the cooling asphalt, feeling the day’s heat retreat into storm drains, and I imagine the earth rotating under me like a slow, dark gear. Somewhere a bakery exhales its first yawning batch of yeast, and the scent reaches me before sound does, a private telegram. I answer by smiling at a cat who refuses to acknowledge me; we are both trespassers in the same moment. I think of all the people I love who are still folded in their dreams, eyelids fluttering like faulty blinds, and I send them each a silent bulletin: the night is almost over, but it has left a bowl of blue milk on every windowsill. Drink, and remember how it felt to be weightless. By the time the sky’s edge bruises to lavender, I will slip back indoors, wipe the city dust from my soles, and pretend I never saw the day being born. But the secret will keep me tall: that for seven uncontested minutes, I owned the horizon, and it did not ask my name.
